titleStmt.title: Letter from Katie Mooney to her brother Peter Mooney, 4 February 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Katie Mooney

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Peter Mooney was born 5 February 1886 in Folistown, Dunboyne, Co Meath. Orphaned at age 7, he and his sister Katie (b. 1884) were raised by their uncle William and his wife Mary in Caulstown. While there are no surviving enlistment papers for Peter, his War Badge Index Record shows his enlistment date as 18 October 1915.
He served with the Royal Artillery and later transferred to the Royal Engineers where he was promoted to the rank of Sergeant. He served in several places at the Western Front, including Ypres, St. Omer and Passchendael. Peter and Kate were avid correspondents. This letter is from a collection of over ninety surviving letters.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Caulstown,
Dunboyne,
Co. Meath. 4th Feb '16 My Dear Peter, I'm Delighted to receive
your ever welcome letter
today containing the good
news that we will see you
next week for certain. I am writing a few lines
to the Governor this evening
telling him that you are
coming next week. I am
sure he will be delighted.
I am going to Dunboyne
this evening to attend a
committee meeting regarding
cookery classes which are
starting next week in the
old School. Our Nurse's
meeting is tomorrow evening
Tom Bracken was married
on last Wednesday. It was
a very nice quiet wedding
no wreaths or veils or bouquets
nor white silk or satin. Mc Carthy sent the carriage
with them to the Chapel.
Miss Walshe gave the wedding
breakfast. Hugh Mullaly was
best man and Lillie Murray
was bridesmaid. They are living in the
front lodge. I wonder
what you will say when
you hear that George Moran
is listed as a private in
the Dublin Fusiliers. Himself
and Little were having a
good time drinking before
this. So I hear that the
old man cleared th him.
Don't say anything about
this if you write to the Paddins
as it wasn't there I heard the
news. I didn't get any word
of how Mrs Farrell was yet.
I expect a letter in the morning.
I wont send the Drogheda
or Sport to morrow, as they
will be here when you come.
Bridget says she will give
you a rare feed wh in the
mornings.
I got a new Tyre and Tube
for my bicycle as the other
one blew up and left me
on the road. Before I
close this letter I must
wish you every joy from
all here as to-day is your
birthday. Many happy
returns of same to you. I have Tobacco here for you
also socks and gloves. I will now say a fond good
bye to you. With best love
from all here. May God
Bless & Protect you is the
sincere prayer of your fond
Aunt & Sister.
